question: Hi there, do you know how to make a citation? I'm not completely sure. Thanks!
reply: Hi ! Welcome to Wikipedia! To add in-line citations, you'll simply surround the reference information using ref tabs, like this: [SOURCE INFORMATION]. Most likely, the sources you'll be using will be from the internet. The best thing to do would be to use the [TEMPLATE_REF] template and then surround that with ref tabs. An example would be [CITATION]. You can review the different parameters that you can use within the cite web template by clicking [TEMPLATE_REF], and you can read more information about citing references in-line by clicking [POLICY]. Please let me know if you have any more questions and I'll be happy to answer them and help you further. :-) Best

question: How do I create a citation
reply: There is a friendly guide [POLICY]. Usually, you'd want the date of the source, the date you accessed the page, the source's title, and the URL if you are citing an online source. If you are trying to cite a book, you would really want to also specify the pages.

question: I don't understand how to find sentences that need to be cited.
reply: RotML Welcome to Wikipedia. You need to find the "[citation needed]" tags that have been added to the ends of various sentences. IF you can find a realiable source for that statement, then replace the tag with the reference that you found. If you don't already know then [POLICY] is a good source for how to add a reference. Happy editing!

question:
Hello!
I need to ask an important question. Please what is a citation first of all?
reply:
@ Hello, and thank you for your interest in editing Wikipedia!
A citation serves to show where a piece of information came from. You can add a citation by clicking the "cite" button on the top left of your screen. After clicking the button, you can input where you learned the information, such as a book and the page number. On Wikipedia, sources should be verifiable and reliable. You can read more here: [POLICY]
A citation may look like this: [CITATION] where the text in red is whatever fact the citation supports. The citation is the small blue box with the number inside. If you click/hover over the icon, it would show the source.
If you would like to add a citation, [HELP_PAGE] would step you though the process. If you are interested, [HELP_PAGE] may also be worth viewing as it gives a general overview of editing.
:Does this help?

question: How do I cite something? I’ve read the tutorial and it didn’t make much sense to me. Many thanks.
reply:
Hey , I'm happy to help as it seems ~delta is taking a break at the moment. Citations are generally placed within tags in the source code of an article, which causes a footnote to appear in the references list at the bottom. Write the reference information within the tags; you can write it in plain text but it is recommended to use a citation template such as [TEMPLATE_REF], which will automatically format the reference for you, among other benefits. As an example, to cite you would insert the following markup into the source: [CITATION]. This generates this citation:[CITATION]
Consider reading [HELP_PAGE] for further information, which is a simplified version of the main [POLICY] guideline. Let me know if you have any questions! —

If you are editing the [POLICY], click on at the top of the edit window, then on . Select the source type (webpage, book...) and fill in information about the source. [HELP_PAGE] explains this better.

question:
Hi,
How to expedite the acceptance of my first draft?
This is the link:
[LINK]
reply: Observer.physio Good day. Please note that all new page reviewers are volunteers who review articles in their own time and based on their interests. There are currently about ten thousand new articles in the backlog. Therefore, it might take a day or two, or even up to three months, for an article to be reviewed. Kindly remain patient in the meantime, and thank you for your contribution. Stay safe.

question: How do I create a draft article?
reply: @ See [POLICY] and Welcome! Creating a new article from scratch is extremely challenging, and new editors are strongly recommended to spend a few months learning how Wikipedia works, by making improvements to some of our existing seven million articles before trying it. When you do decide to have a go at a new article, you are highly encouraged to read [POLICY]. If you haven't already also check out [POLICY]; it's a lot of fun! Happy editing! ~ (he/him • )

question: The article is in draft. Please guide me how to publish it from draft?

question: Hi Cullen. I want to use the Draft function in the following way. Rather than produce a draft from scratch, I want the draft's starting point to be an existing article, such as the Ethics article. Can that be done?
reply: Hello, ForeverBetter. That is not the purpose of draft space, which is to draft new articles. You can use your personal sandbox space to draft new content for existing articles. As for [WIKILINK], it is not a good idea in almost all cases to do a dramatic rewrite of an article that many editors have contributed to over 24 years. Perhaps you should describe your plans for the article at [WIKILINK] first.

question: I am practicing in my sandbox visual editor and I can't seem to save without publishing. if I close out and go back it recovers "Unsaved Changes" but I have no idea how to save my changes without publishing. Everything is saying there should be a "Save changes" button somewhere but the only thing I see is a "Publish button"
reply: On Wikipedia, 'save changes' means 'publish'. Sandboxes are never indexed by search engines and it is unlikely anyone will look at a personal one unless they have a reason to. Therefore, it's ok to put [POLICY] there. If you really want to write things and save them without publishing them to Wikipedia, you can write in a word processor and copy/paste it later.

question: I created a page for Pioneer Bank in the sandbox. I would like to make it live, please let me know how I can accomplish that.
reply:
Hello PioneerBank,
Since it seems to be the case that you have a [POLICY] in regards to the subject of this page, I have added an "[POLICY]" template to the top of the page so that you might have it reviewed by another editor before publication. It is unlikely to be accepted in this state, though, as many if not all of the references used are not [POLICY] of the subject, and much of the information about branch locations and operating hours are considered to be out of the scope of Wikipedia and will likely be deleted or ignored. I advise reviewing our guidelines on what makes a [POLICY] and find information from said reliable sources that discuss Pioneer Bank.
Also, you will have to change your username, since it implies that you are not editing as one person, but on behalf of the entire bank.

Hard luck, @. It sounds like you didn't save your work. You have to hit the "publish" button to do so. question:
Hi Bookku,
I've created a new page for my business named 'Sintela'. After creating it in the sandbox I moved it over to my main page and published it. However I cannot see it online. Has it gone for review and if so how long does this process normally take before it appears online?
Kind regards, David
reply: @ First of all, you really need to change your username before you get blocked for having a company name as your username (it may imply you are sharing your account; this has happened in the past). Secondly, your "main page" that you're referring to is actually your userpage, and not articlespace. Just as well, as there are image copyright and neutrality issues. I moved the sandbox to draftspace, and had the userpage speedily deleted.
